sentences.text: Just let me go.
Finish, he--
As as a matter of fact-- as a matter of fact-- I don't think he's-- he asked me how many officers like you are here?
And when he asked me this question, I realized he is coming from the direction where he has already seen two heroes like myself.
So I decided on the spot to tell him the truth.
I said, we are four, but we didn't have accommodation.
We couldn't get together all four, so I had two of us there and two of us there.
He looked at me with such, you know, a mocking look.
And to say, I have seen already two like you, two heroes.
You are the other two.
He let me go.
And we arrived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lviv, or Lemberg, I should call it.
Yiddish.
I was in Lviv a few days only.
One thing that stands out in my mind with Lviv was there was one synagogue left.
Lviv had tens of thousands of Jews-- tens of thousands.
It was a big city with many Jews.
There was one synagogue.
And when I arrived, that was, what, five minutes-- five months after the end of the war, the walls of the synagogue were still with blood-- blooded, spots of blood.
And we were told by people that they went up with children, throw them down, and said, let your Jewish God catch you.
I just-- what I remember seeing was the blood spots on the--
On the walls of the synagogue.
On the wall.
That was, what is it, five months after the war.
No.
The first survivors I started meeting was I went over to Krakow.
From Lviv.
To cross the border at that time was no problem.
Just a train or something.
I don't remember exact.
But I know there was-- there was no-- once I got out this-- this-- train police, there was no problems.
As a matter of fact, you see, it was such a flux, everything.
People came back from the war, came back from concentration camps, that it was very little check.
I arrived in Krakow, which was already over the border from the Soviets.
I don't remember Krakow.
I found a city completely untouched.
Just there.
No Jews was there-- a few hundred thousand-- not a few hundred-- wait, wait.
The Jewish community was nothing.
Just the city existed without the Jews.
This is Schindler's city, by the way.
I went from Krakow to Lodz.
Now, in Lodz, was already a center [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for Jewish refugees.
Lodz before the war had a quarter of a million Jews.
Nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Nothing.
Nothing.
Just survivors who came from different places from partisan-- people were in the woods, you know, hiding.
In Lodz was just big, large rooms.
I remember the address.
This was where all Jewish people-- so it was all-- it was enough, one house, to house, all the survivors.
One house.
A large house.
For all of us, yes.
Yes, absolutely, yes.
[NON-ENGLISH] 16.
To translate it, the-- the Holly Jacob.
Holly Jacob Street. 16 Holly Jacob Street.
Yes.
In the hundreds.
Yes.
Young men.
Less women, of course.
I would say that men survive more than women.
I would say so.
Logically, it is not right, because it's easy to recognize a Jew, a man than a woman, for obvious reasons.
But from what I remember, there were more men survivors than women.
I think so, at least.
This was-- this was organized already.
This was organized by Joint.
--stop from Soviet Russia.
First outside Jewish world after--
Yes.
No.
Lodz.
I cannot say for sure.
Joint, Jewish agency, the Bricha.
You know what Bricha is, yes?
These were the organizations.
And besides, there was another thing.
The Jews are very political minded, as you may well know.
After the whole Holocaust, immediately start popping off the political organizations-- religious one, leftist, rightist.
You know, oh from 100 people, 1,000 parties.
This was typical of Jewish people.
Yes, starting.
Very beginning.
Yes.
The very beginning.
Yes, yes.
Jewish life started in a negative way starting-- I don't know if you can see, but it was starting suddenly, you know, like after a fire, you see bits of grass.
It's like it came out.
This is how I saw it.
Suddenly you see political parties that were-- it was anachronism, because it was ridiculous after all of this.
I don't remember exactly.
But I remember going from Lodz to Warsaw.
Warsaw was disaster, one disaster.
Arriving in Warsaw was a shocking experience, because I learned in Warsaw.
I spent my childhood in Warsaw.
I knew every corner of it, and nothing.
Absolutely nothing.
Jewish, non-Jewish as well, because there was a second uprising.
You have to realize there was a Jewish uprising, and a year later was a Polish uprising.
And what the Germans didn't succeed burning the first time they finished off the second time.
Actually, all you could see was rubble, pieces of brick.
No, nothing, nothing, nothing.
Single buildings that were just like a chimney.
A waste land.
That's the right description.
Yes.
It was ridiculous.
How can you find-- you-- if somebody will tell you here was the yeshiva, you'd wonder why.
It's a brick.
Nothing.
Nothing was left.
Nothing-- no orientation whatsoever.
What, where, what?
Nothing.
You couldn't even tell which part of Warsaw vaguely.
No, nothing.
Absolutely.
Where I reached out-- reached out, just-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I don't remember exactly if this is what.
But in Warsaw, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you know what Judenrat is?
The Judenrat in Warsaw was a certain address.
The Judenrat--
In a building that survived.
Yes, yes, yes.
Till today.
For some reason, I don't know.
Maybe the Germans wanted to make a museum to keep Jews alive and to show them through what-- what a Jew looked like when he's alive.
I-- I think I went there.
I'm not sure.
But I think I went there.
Anyway, I got a contact in Warsaw.
And seeing that I have got nothing to do in Poland, we heard that there was displaced persons camps in Germany.
It's a funny thing, but after all this-- after all the concentration of Jews were in Germany after the war.
The survivors tended to go.
Why, I don't know.
The Joint worked-- the Joint worked in Germany.
And [INAUDIBLE] near Munich was a large camp.
Don't mistake it.
It wasn't a concentration.
It was a camp for displaced persons.
And while I was in-- while I was in Warsaw, I got in contact with some religious Jews.
I wasn't religious at all.
But they-- I knew their language.
I decided I will take them for a ride.
So they took me for a ride, and they gave me the contact.
And I went over Czechoslovakia.
But here, I must stop for one minute.
This case that I would like to stress, this is an interesting thing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the way somewhere-- I don't know where or when-- I was in a city called Breslau, Wroclaw in Polish.
This was Prussia before the war, Germany.
While I was walking around there on my way somewhere-- I don't know-- a Polish policeman pulled me up, called me here, took me to the police station, and gave me a good beating.
Not for what.
Didn't ask me.
Didn't talk to me.
Didn't accuse me of anything.
He didn't have what to accuse me.
But while he was beating me up, an officer arrived, and he told him to stop.
That was Poland again, I would call it.
A chapter.
I was lucky, because it's well known there was pogroms, and Jews were killed.
There was a city called Kielce, and 43 survivors were massacred in one day.
A beating was relatively fortunate.
I have to be grateful.
Yes.
But this was-- I knew I am in Poland.
This was a greeting, a Polish greeting, I would call it.
Welcome home, yes.
And they asked me if I speak Polish.
Anyway, I went from Warsaw--
Breslau.
I don't remember now, was I in Breslau?
No, that was before I reached Warsaw, because from Warsaw I went straight over the border with a group already, organized by the Bricha.
And we arrived.
We went through Karlsruhe, a well-known place, as I saw in Czechoslovakia.
And then we came to Regensburg, and Regensburg to Munich.
In Munich was already a big-- a large building where displaced persons were-- all kind-- Jews, non-Jews, all kinds of people.
Absolutely it's impossible to establish.
I have got no contact whatsoever.
No way of knowing.
All I know, just stories from reading what happened here, what happened there.
When-- because the made-- the Germans had-- every few months they made [GERMAN] it's called in German.
The made-- how shall I describe it-- an event, taking first the poor ones, and the intellectuals, and then every time-- and you know.
So somewhere in September of '42 was the last time when Jews were taken out from that place and [INAUDIBLE].
And got to the end.
And that's it.
No.
Munich was Munich.
No.
This was not a camp.
This is a large building.
And God knows, maybe the Gestapo was there before.
A very large building--
A big building in Munich itself.
A big building, yes.
In Munich itself.
No, not necessarily.
There were all kinds of people there from all over the world.
Waiting out.
Refugees, yes.
Waiting out.
Not necessarily refugees, no.
There I was approached by a woman who was a survivor from Warsaw ghetto, from the last, the ones who went out in the-- on the-- on the--
Yes.
Her name was Krysza.
Last of the survivors.
She was a-- a very fighting woman, yes.
Small but very energetic type person.
I remember her going around, just looking.
Who, where, when-- until she came to me and asked me who I am, what I was, and where I would like to go.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she gave me a choice of two camps.
One was Landsberg.
Landsberg is a well-known place.
Hitler, as a matter of fact, after his putsch, the Munich Putsch in 1923-- it's a well-known event-- sat there in jail in Landsberg.
Anyway, she asked me, what-- would I like to go there at a Jewish camp in Landsberg or Fernwald?
And for some reason, I don't know why I said Fernwald, and I met my wife.
So you went to Fernwald.
And joined a kibbutz.
In kibbutz there were so many people-- I told you, the political groupings started.
There was the rightists, and as I always was more to the left oriented.
So I took the [NON-ENGLISH], I would call it now.
You see, not extreme left, but--
Socialist.
Yeah, socialist type.
Yes.
First week on a Friday evening.
Ah, this I can remember.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was an event.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know.
This story has to be told from my, in my opinion, from two people, not from one.
Because you have to realize, when I met her, she met me.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] In a nutshell, I was very impressed.
I think that would be the most diplomatic way of describing.
Yes.
Very impressed.
Till June '43.
From January '46 till-- a month-- a year and a half.
Nah.
I was single.
Not with me.
In the same camp, yes.
In the same camp.
That's what I mean.
Her parents died here in Australia.
Yes.
June '47.
We went to Palestine.
There was no Israel at that time.
Yes.
Nobody forced me.
Nobody forced anybody.
When one is young, one is very idealistic.
Just to Haifa.
I went by with
On the yes.
I left Germany.
I went to France.
To France.
Yes, Rachel and her parents, yes.
Yes.
4,500 people.
Not-- not far from Marseilles, I would say.
A place-- no, Saint-Chamas.
How do you pronounce it?
How do you spell it in French?
I cannot tell you.
Near Marseilles, yes.
Yes.
I remember, the impression of Saint-Chamas going through Marseilles, that was the first time in my life I've seen the sea.
I was 23 years old.
I've never seen a sea.
And suddenly there's so much water.
I remember, I was engulfed, you see.
I didn't know.
Your breath was taken away--
Yes.
Yes, yes.
For a couple of weeks.
It was organized by the Jewish agency.
And then off to
was a boat that was travelling on the Mississippi.
A river boat that was equipped for this trip.
Fitted out-- bunks one, two, three, four five, I think it was.
You had to slide in, ride like this.
A wooden-- a wooden boat.
Of course.
A wooden boat.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the conditions were terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The conditions were terrible, because there was not much air.
But I luckily-- you see, sometimes I have got-- I was resourceful.
I-- I don't remember how, but I was on the deck organizing people.
Every time people were coming out to having air for-- for a while, and changing.
And I was the fellow who was standing outside organizing it.
Traffic policeman.
So I was in the air all the time.
And sleeping, I was in the hospital under the bed of some sick person, not sleeping in the benches.
So [?
I had ?] it.
This is  a historical-- now you are coming not to a private story but history.
It was a historical event.
Yes.
This was the largest illegal ship before the establishment of state of Israel that tried to approach in an illegal way-- so-called.
Well, the Jews didn't recognize illegal.
In a so-called illegal way, enter into Israel.
Only survivors.
Yes, yes, yes.
On the way, as a matter of fact, some pregnant women died.
Because the conditions were so terrible.
Because conditions-- I remember being outside, always outside, I remember how they were tossed into the sea.
The bodies?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, you-- you had your-- your traffic officer's job on board the The left Marseilles.
Yes, on the way to Palestine, to Haifa.
And about a day before arriving to Haifa--
I'm not sure, but I would say 10 days as a guess.
Yes, I was, on a guess.
Yes.
I don't remember suffering from lack of food on No, that was organized by the Jewish agency.
And water, yes, yes.
The only thing was the amenities were very bad, because you couldn't cater for so many people.
Huge over-crowding, yes.
A day out from Haifa, the English.
The English were shadowing us all the time.
Yes.
I don't know English warships.
You could see the color of a warship is different than an ordinary ship.
You see, you could see warship?
It's grayish, the same you see in Australia.
It's always all over the world the same.
A warship is different than the other one.
Yes, [INAUDIBLE] all the time from the very beginning.
From Marseilles, yes.
Let's say a few hours after.
The-- the intelligence was quite OK.
They knew that we were coming out, trying to break the blockade.
In Haifa, the English approached us.
And the loudspeaker is telling us that we are not permitted to enter Haifa, Palestine.
July.
I don't know dates.
July definitely.
Date I couldn't tell you.
Yes.
Yes.
The middle of a hot summer, yes.
The British?
Well, the-- the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people who organized the ship were the Haganah.
They started throwing-- when they approached, they started throwing on them vegetable.
There was no exchange of fire, of course.
But they approached us, of course, and then made a dent in the ship, and stopped it, and came in the-- there was a bit of a struggle.
A few people were killed, and a number were injured.
Boarded the boat, yes.
And took us in tow to Haifa.
In Haifa, there was not a person to be seen, because the British announced that nobody should get out of their homes.
A curfew.
--landed in Haifa, not seeing one person civil, except the British army.
We were taken in-- in three ships, 1,500 on each.
In the same day.
In the same day.
In the same day.
That's correct.
A boat, it was-- that was not made for people, for passengers.
It was just a deep hull, you know, and people were in there.
And I somehow again organized myself to be on top.
I don't remember how.
I don't know.
But I-- I was lying on top in the boat.
I don't know how.
--where there was a bit of air.
That's correct.
The same place.
Same ship.
On the ship?
Very bad.
It was hot.
It was July.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No change of clothes.
No water-- I mean, for washing, yes.
I will tell you, coming from Russia, you have to realize that everything pales.
So I-- personally, I don't see them as very bad.
I don't have bad experience.
I will tell you why.
I had very little knowledge of English.
In Russian, you couldn't speak to them.
Neither German.
Very little knowledge.
But a few words I started commanding.
And being on top, I approached where there was-- the kitchen was.
And I told them, I want to go in there.
I don't-- under what pretext-- pretext.
And I got back with some [?
Nestles, ?] milk.
A bit they gave me, and a bit I took myself.
Not too bad.
It happened we were for two months on the sea-- from July till September, when we arrived back in Germany.
Sailing around the Mediterranean.

Because we crossed the Gibraltar, we-- we were in France, where they tried to organize if people want to live-- live in-- that was at that time, it was a struggle between Bevan, the minister of foreign affairs, and Clement Attlee's government, who was very anti-Jewish, very much so.
Water, no problem.
Food was a problem.
Hungry, yes.
We got the biscuits.
We were-- all biscuits that you could see.
That no more illegal Jewish migrants should go to Palestine.
At that time, there was a struggle between the Arabs and the Jews, and the British in the middle.
In Germany, from Germany, all over the world.
They succeeded to a degree-- only to a degree.
That's correct.
Till September.
In September, we came to a camp called Poppendorf.
That was not far-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were taken out-- we were-- our boat arrived in Hamburg.
And that camp was not far from a city, a well-known city called Lubeck, a German city.
[INAUDIBLE] in a camp.
But this time a camp, it surrounded by military personnel not to be able to get out.
A real camp.
With barbed wire.
